summary:
The primary focus here should be identifying which single hormone fits best with the description "under inhibitory control." Prolactin meets these criteria through its regulation via dopaminergic pathways originating at the level of the hypothalamus. Other endocrine functions may involve both stimulatory and suppressive elements but don't match precisely what was asked for regarding pure inhibition driven primarily or exclusively by a particular region such as the hypothalamic area involved directly controlling prolactin release negatively without needing additional activating signals beyond initial suppression efforts initiated there itself.
The main point revolves around understanding hormonal feedback loops where one gland influences another indirectly; however, when pinpointing direct negative controls exerted centrally over peripheral glands' activities—like those seen classically between brain regions themselves—the answer clearly narrows down based purely upon known physiological facts presented initially within context too. Agent1 effectively highlighted why prolactin alone satisfies conditions outlined concerning exclusive reliance on central nervous system input leading towards reduced output levels observed clinically due simply because more complex interactions weren’t necessary nor explicitly stated needed elsewhere outside core requirement parameters set forth originally during problem formulation stage prior discussion began today. To summarize succinctly:

Prolactin production is strictly inhibited by dopamine released from the hypothalamus, making it distinctively controlled compared to other hormones mentioned whose regulations might include multiple
